title: Orphan rule: daily_stats — locked but never accessed by the client
detail: The rule `match /daily_stats/{statId} { allow read, write: if false; }` exists in firestore.rules with the comment 'unused collection — locked', but no client code in src/ reads or writes the 'daily_stats' collection (the only historical reference is a commented-out setDailyStats(null) in DailyStatistics.jsx). Same orphan pattern as shift_logs — no runtime impact but misleading. Confirm no Cloud Function still depends on it before removing.

title: Missing composite index: tasks — assignedUserId + timerStatus
detail: `pauseOtherTasks` issues `query(collection(db, 'tasks'), where('assignedUserId', '==', userId), where('timerStatus', '==', 'running'))`. This is a compound equality query on two different fields. Firestore requires a composite index for this; neither field pair appears in firestore.indexes.json. At runtime this throws FAILED_PRECONDITION ('The query requires an index') every time a worker starts a new task while another is running — the guard that is supposed to pause the other task silently fails. The error is swallowed by the outer `try/catch` (line 296) and logged only to console.error, so the duplicate running-task state that this guard exists to prevent can persist undetected.

title: Missing composite index: request_notifications — recipientId + isRead
detail: `query(collection(db, 'request_notifications'), where('recipientId', '==', currentUser.uid), where('isRead', '==', false))` is a compound query on two different fields. No composite index for `recipientId + isRead` exists in firestore.indexes.json. This listener (and the identical one in ManagerNotifications.jsx:141-144) fires on mount for every logged-in user. The FAILED_PRECONDITION error from the missing index causes the unread-notification count (the bell badge) and the task-notification card list to stay permanently empty — a silent failure, because the onSnapshot error callback only logs to console.error and does not surface a UI error. The ManagerNotifications listener additionally mutes the time-extension beep that depends on this count.

title: Missing composite index: request_notifications — recipientId + isRead (ManagerNotifications duplicate)
detail: Identical compound query `where('recipientId', '==', currentUser.uid), where('isRead', '==', false)` on `request_notifications` as in NotificationsContext.jsx. Same missing index, same FAILED_PRECONDITION risk. Both listeners are live simultaneously for manager-role users: the manager sees the notification bell AND the notifications panel — both go dark when the index is absent. This query is also the source of the count that triggers the audible task-extension beep (ManagerNotifications.jsx:155-158), so the sound also stops firing.

title: Missing composite index: calendar_requests — createdAt range (reportData + CalendarChangeHistory)
detail: `query(collection(db, 'calendar_requests'), where('createdAt', '>=', startStr), where('createdAt', '<=', endStr), orderBy('createdAt', 'desc'))` combines two inequalities on the same field with an orderBy on that same field. Firestore serves same-field range + orderBy from the automatic single-field index, so ordinarily this would be fine. However, because the same collection also carries an `array-contains` query pattern elsewhere (`where('managerIds', 'array-contains', uid)`) and Firestore must pick one index, the comment in firestore.indexes.json says 'Firestore emits an exact create-index link at runtime for any composite this file misses — create those during testing'. No entry for `calendar_requests` on `createdAt` + `createdAt` exists. At production scale, if Firestore's query planner decides a composite is needed (which it will if the collection grows large), this will throw FAILED_PRECONDITION silently (reportData.js wraps it in a try/catch at line 82 that swallows the error to `crDocs = []`), causing the reschedule-discipline metrics in the monthly export to come up blank without any user-visible error. Also affects CalendarChangeHistory.jsx where the error is surfaced as a generic Lithuanian string rather than a hint about the missing index.

title: text-xs + opacity-70/80 drops session-label contrast below 4.5:1
detail: The session label row and the start/planned-time sub-labels are rendered at `text-xs` (12 px — the hard floor) with `opacity-70` on `text-ink` color. Gray-500 (#6B7280) on a white card is 4.6:1 at full opacity; at 0.7 that falls to ~3.2:1, failing WCAG AA 4.5:1 for normal text (§7). Workers read this card continuously while a session runs.

title: Multiple ManagerNotifications notification cards use text-xs + opacity-80 for readable content
detail: Several notification card types (new-user, session-edit, error-report, task-created) render informational paragraphs — reason text, comment quotes, email addresses, instructions — at `text-xs opacity-80`. ink-muted (#6B7280) on white is 4.6:1 at 100% opacity; 0.80 opacity brings it to ~3.7:1, a WCAG AA failure. These are manager-facing action cards with decision copy, not decorative glyphs. Lines 889, 918, 963, 996, 1157, and 1221 all exhibit this pattern.

title: DailyStatistics session-timeline table renders at text-xs with no card fallback on mobile
detail: The inner worker detail modal renders a `<div class='hidden overflow-x-auto md:block'>` table for the day timeline (line 1464), but that table's rows use `text-xs` (12 px at-floor) throughout (line 1478). Worse, unlike the top-level task lists which gate on a JS-derived `viewMode` state, this timeline table uses a static Tailwind `hidden md:block` breakpoint. On a phone in portrait the mobile-card alternative (lines 1384-1461) is shown instead, which is correct — but the table rows' `text-xs` class will be seen on tablets (768 px) that still have limited viewport width and would benefit from `text-caption` (the token alias for 12 px with 16 px line-height). More critically the table also uses `text-sm` (`text-sm` = 14 px) for some cells (line 1326 in the summary table), so there is inconsistency between sections — the session timeline table hard-codes `text-xs` while the summary table uses `text-sm`.

title: DailyStatistics MobileStatsCard description and confirm-status labels use text-xs
detail: Inside `MobileStatsCard` (the mobile task card for the stats view) the task description paragraph (line 2005) uses `text-xs text-ink-muted`, the confirmation-status label (lines 2052, 2057) uses `text-xs font-medium`, and the comment-count badge inside an `IconButton` child (line 2083) uses `text-xs font-bold`. These all fall at the 12 px floor with no breathing room. The description in particular renders multi-line prose at 12 px on a phone screen — a legibility failure for workers outdoors (§5 §7).

title: Comment timestamps in TaskDetailsModals render at text-xs (12 px floor, no margin)
detail: `<span class='text-xs text-ink-muted'>` renders the full locale datetime string of each comment. At 12 px this is right at the DESIGN_SYSTEM §5 hard floor with no upward margin, and the string is dense (date + time). The design system explicitly calls out the 8 px comment timestamp as a known failing (§7 Contrast). This is the surviving instance — it should be `text-caption` (12 px/16 px via the token) at minimum, or `text-body` for the primary copy.

title: AllUsersCalendar desktop drill-down bar has a ~42px hit target, 2px short of 44px AA floor
detail: The code comment explicitly documents that the transparent ::before pseudo-element extends the visible h-6 (24 px) pill to ~42 px total tappable height (`before:-inset-y-[9px]` adds +9 px each side). WCAG AA and DESIGN_SYSTEM §7 both require ≥44 px. The comment says 'toward the 44 px AA floor' — it does not reach it. Changing `-inset-y-[9px]` to `-inset-y-[10px]` would give exactly 44 px.
